3: Over the past century, the use of synthetic fertilizers rich in nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) has enabled an unprecedented boost in crop and food production for an increasing population. Global N demand for fertilizer production has increased 278-458 times since the patenting of the Haber-Bosch process in 1908 (Sutton et al., 2013). For

P, an increase of 88-130 times has been estimated since 1900 (Cordell and White, 2014). Similarly, the global K production increased 19-fold between 1929 (U.S. Department of Commerce, 1933) and 2022 (USGS, 2022b). This excessive nutrient use in a leaky agricultural system causes environmental impacts that contribute to the transgression of planetary boundaries (Richardson et al., 2023) and impair the achievement of the sustainable development goals of the United Nations

(United Nations, 2015). The Haber-Bosch process is responsible for 1.2% of the global CO2-eq. emissions (Smith et al., 2020) and approximately 50% of the global reactive N production (Galloway et al., 2004). About 80% of reactive N (worth 200 billion US$) is lost to the environment every year, contributing to the triple planetary crisis of climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution (UNEP, 2023). To produce mineral P and K fertilizers, raw materials are mined from non-renewable phosphate rock (USGS, 2022a) and evaporite deposits (USGS, 2022b), causing eutrophication, air and water pollution, land degradation, and soil contamination through cadmium and uranium (de Boer et al., 2019). In addition, many countries depend on imported fertilizers. In Europe, 30% of the N, 68% of the P, and 85% of the K consumed are imported, which threatens the stability of food prices (European Union, 2022). This transportation from the production sites to the different marketplaces has been shown to have a significant impact on acidification and resource depletion (Hasler et al., 2015), as well as on global warming (Meisterling et al., 2009), toxicity, and ozone formation (Zhou et al., 2021).

Integrated farming systems that blend conventional with organic practices have been proposed as an environmentally friendly alternative to conventional farming that delivers equally or more nutritious food while providing greater ecosystem services and social benefits (Rega- nold and Wachter, 2016). Organic fertilizer application can for example contribute to soil organic carbon (SOC) accumulation, lower soil bulk density, and increase porosity, water holding capacity, soil pH, and N and P availability (Bilong et al., 2022). The C storage in soils plays an important role in increasing food security and combating climate change (Le Foll, 2022). The European Farm to Fork Strategy, being at the forefront of sustainable agriculture, aims at reducing nutrient losses by 50% and fertilizer use by 20% until 2030 (compared to 1990 levels), while targeting to bring at least 25% of the EU's agricultural land under organic farming (European Commission, 2020b). The EU Circular Economy Action Plan also proposes the development of an Integrated Nutrient Management Plan to stimulate the markets for recovered nu- trients (European Commission, 2020a). Furthermore, the international

"4 per 1000" Initiative aims to increase the level of C stored in soils by 0.4% per year (equivalent to a global average of 0.6 tonnes of C per hectare per year) to offset annual greenhouse gas emissions originating from fossil fuels (Minasny et al., 2017). Through these initiatives, together with the new Fertilizing Products Regulation, which provides a legal basis for the use of recycled materials in fertilizers (European Commission, 2018), the EU expects an increase in the demand for organic fertilizers - those containing organic C (min. 15% for solid and 5% for liquid organic fertilizers, fresh matter content) and nutrients (the sum of N, P2O5, and K2O fresh matter contents shall be at least 4% for solid and 3% for liquid organic fertilizers) of exclusively biological origin (European Council, 2019).

Common organic fertilizers are animal- or plant-based and include livestock manure (fresh or dried), meals from residual animal tissues and carcasses (such as meat, bones, horns, etc.), compost, green manure (plants ploughed under to enrich the soil), as well as other by-products from the agrifood industry (e.g. meals generated during vegetable oil production, molasses from sugar production). Although these fertilizers seem promising, their role in feeding a growing population remains controversial (Barbieri et al., 2021). They often have low and variable nutrient concentrations (max. fresh matter content of 14% N, 7% P, and 6% K, Table 1) compared to mineral fertilizers (max. 82% N, 55% P, and 62% K) (IFA, 2023), which makes the transport and application more challenging (and expensive). Most of these nutrients are in the organic form (principally not directly plant available) and their microbial mineralization depends on temperature, water content, soil properties, and type of product (Geisseler et al., 2021). Their production is in some cases linked to land use and land use change, as is the case for soybean meal (Castanheira and Freire, 2013), and they might compete with other higher-value products (e.g. fishmeal can be used as food and feed) (FAO, 2022b). The application of certain organic fertilizers is also limited by regulations, such as the EU Nitrates Regulation, which limits farms in nitrate-vulnerable zones to apply a maximum of 170 kg N/ha/y from livestock manure. Especially the replacement of mineral fertilizers with waste-derived alternatives presents a challenge, requiring consideration

"4 per 1000" Initiative aims to increase the level of C stored in soils by 0.4% per year (equivalent to a global average of 0.6 tonnes of C per hectare per year) to offset annual greenhouse gas emissions originating from fossil fuels (Minasny et al., 2017). Through these initiatives, together with the new Fertilizing Products Regulation, which provides a legal basis for the use of recycled materials in fertilizers (European Commission, 2018), the EU expects an increase in the demand for organic fertilizers - those containing organic C (min. 15% for solid and 5% for liquid organic fertilizers, fresh matter content) and nutrients (the sum of N, P2O5, and K2O fresh matter contents shall be at least 4% for solid and 3% for liquid organic fertilizers) of exclusively biological origin (European Council, 2019).

Common organic fertilizers are animal- or plant-based and include livestock manure (fresh or dried), meals from residual animal tissues and carcasses (such as meat, bones, horns, etc.), compost, green manure (plants ploughed under to enrich the soil), as well as other by-products from the agrifood industry (e.g. meals generated during vegetable oil production, molasses from sugar production). Although these fertilizers seem promising, their role in feeding a growing population remains controversial (Barbieri et al., 2021). They often have low and variable nutrient concentrations (max. fresh matter content of 14% N, 7% P, and 6% K, Table 1) compared to mineral fertilizers (max. 82% N, 55% P, and 62% K) (IFA, 2023), which makes the transport and application more challenging (and expensive). Most of these nutrients are in the organic form (principally not directly plant available) and their microbial mineralization depends on temperature, water content, soil properties, and type of product (Geisseler et al., 2021). Their production is in some cases linked to land use and land use change, as is the case for soybean meal (Castanheira and Freire, 2013), and they might compete with other higher-value products (e.g. fishmeal can be used as food and feed) (FAO, 2022b). The application of certain organic fertilizers is also limited by regulations, such as the EU Nitrates Regulation, which limits farms in nitrate-vulnerable zones to apply a maximum of 170 kg N/ha/y from livestock manure. Especially the replacement of mineral fertilizers with waste-derived alternatives presents a challenge, requiring consideration

of user expectations, efficiency, complex supply chains, and scaling is- sues (Spiller et al., 2024).

If the demand for organic fertilizers will increase in the future, it is important to understand the environmental impacts of these fertilizers to avoid unexpected consequences. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable and widely accepted method for the evaluation of these im- pacts. LCA studies comparing organic to conventional agriculture including but not limited to fertilizer choice - often show a better environmental performance for organic products per unit of land occupied, but a worse performance per unit of product (due to lower yields) (Meier et al., 2015). To the knowledge of the authors, there are only a few comparative assessments of major groups of organic fertil- izers (Avadí, 2020; Litskas, 2023), and comparability between LCA studies is highly limited due to different assumptions and methods applied. For instance, the functional unit (FU) of LCA studies on bio-based fertilizers often refer to the mass of nutrients (N or P), or total mass/volume of the final product (Egas et al., 2023). Since nutrient ratios vary between organic fertilizers, such a FU does not reflect the fertilizers' actual functionality. Moreover, most available LCAs comparing organic and conventional agricultural products apply an attributional modelling approach (Meier et al., 2015) to study the environmental impact of an average product. Instead, consequential modelling is more appropriate when investigating the environmental consequences of a change in the system, and it is the recommended method when using LCA for policy-making or strategic environmental planning (Weidema et al., 2018).

Consequential modelling also attempts to reflect the economic re- alities behind the supply and demand of goods. Commonly used organic fertilizers are often constrained by resource availability. Manures, for example, are low-priced by-products produced along with a determining product (e.g. meat or milk). In the case of an increased demand for manure, the farmers will not be able to change their production vol- umes. The new suppliers that will absorb this demand are those that can still increase their production capacity for a functionally similar prod- uct, or marginal suppliers. In the case of fertilizers, mineral fertilizer producers are the marginal suppliers. Hence, demanding an extra unit of manure will have the same environmental impact as demanding an extra unit of mineral fertilizer (Fig. 1).

An exception to the constrained nature of by-products occurs when these are not fully utilized (i.e. unconstrained). In that case, the demand for an additional unit can be satisfied by untapped by-products, and the waste management company becomes the marginal supplier (Weidema, 2000). For example, a novel, currently underexploited, source for organic fertilizers is dried microbial biomass produced when treating industrial wastewaters with low levels of undesired contaminants (e.g. heavy metals), for instance from the food and beverage industry (Spa- noghe et al., 2020). The high nutrient concentrations in these waste- waters (up to 3000 mg/L of N and 600 mg/L of P) present a resource recovery opportunity, not only for N, P, and K, but also for C (Falletti et al., 2014). Microbial fertilizer types that have been considered are based on Aerobic Heterotrophic Bacteria (AHB), Microalgae and AHB (MaB), and Purple Non-Sulphur Bacteria (PNSB) - AHB and MaB pro- duction technologies are commercially available, although the micro- bial biomass is currently not valorised as fertilizer, while PNSB is currently produced in pilot scale (Spiller et al., 2020). Like most organic fertilizers, microbial fertilizers can contribute to replenish the organic matter content in the soil (Sedlar et al., 2023) and allow a slow nutrient release, potentially avoiding nutrient losses (Shaji et al., 2021). Microbial fertilizers have also been shown to improve crop quality (Coppens et al., 2016) and health (Wambacq et al., 2022), compared to other organic fertilizers.

This study aims to quantify the environmental impact of replacing mineral NPK fertilizers with 14 conventional and 3 innovative organic fertilizers, accounting for market constraints through consequential LCA. A comparative assessment of this extent had never been carried out before for fertilizer products. The chosen multi-substance FU includes N,

P, and K to ensure a fair comparison. As there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, the second objective was to assess the contribution of the different organic fertilizers to the achievement of the goal of the "4 per 1000" Initiative. This paper will both analyse the environmental impact of fertilizers and also inform LCA practitioners about the intricacies of using the consequential Ecoinvent database to assess fertilizer products.

This study aims to quantify the environmental impact of replacing mineral NPK fertilizers with 14 conventional and 3 innovative organic fertilizers, accounting for market constraints through consequential LCA. A comparative assessment of this extent had never been carried out before for fertilizer products. The chosen multi-substance FU includes N,

P, and K to ensure a fair comparison. As there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, the second objective was to assess the contribution of the different organic fertilizers to the achievement of the goal of the "4 per 1000" Initiative. This paper will both analyse the environmental impact of fertilizers and also inform LCA practitioners about the intricacies of using the consequential Ecoinvent database to assess fertilizer products.

If the demand for organic fertilizers will increase in the future, it is important to understand the environmental impacts of these fertilizers to avoid unexpected consequences. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable and widely accepted method for the evaluation of these im- pacts. LCA studies comparing organic to conventional agriculture including but not limited to fertilizer choice - often show a better environmental performance for organic products per unit of land occupied, but a worse performance per unit of product (due to lower yields) (Meier et al., 2015). To the knowledge of the authors, there are only a few comparative assessments of major groups of organic fertil- izers (Avadí, 2020; Litskas, 2023), and comparability between LCA studies is highly limited due to different assumptions and methods applied. For instance, the functional unit (FU) of LCA studies on bio-based fertilizers often refer to the mass of nutrients (N or P), or total mass/volume of the final product (Egas et al., 2023). Since nutrient ratios vary between organic fertilizers, such a FU does not reflect the fertilizers' actual functionality. Moreover, most available LCAs comparing organic and conventional agricultural products apply an attributional modelling approach (Meier et al., 2015) to study the environmental impact of an average product. Instead, consequential modelling is more appropriate when investigating the environmental consequences of a change in the system, and it is the recommended method when using LCA for policy-making or strategic environmental planning (Weidema et al., 2018).

Consequential modelling also attempts to reflect the economic re- alities behind the supply and demand of goods. Commonly used organic fertilizers are often constrained by resource availability. Manures, for example, are low-priced by-products produced along with a determining product (e.g. meat or milk). In the case of an increased demand for manure, the farmers will not be able to change their production vol- umes. The new suppliers that will absorb this demand are those that can still increase their production capacity for a functionally similar prod- uct, or marginal suppliers. In the case of fertilizers, mineral fertilizer producers are the marginal suppliers. Hence, demanding an extra unit of manure will have the same environmental impact as demanding an extra unit of mineral fertilizer (Fig. 1).

An exception to the constrained nature of by-products occurs when these are not fully utilized (i.e. unconstrained). In that case, the demand for an additional unit can be satisfied by untapped by-products, and the waste management company becomes the marginal supplier (Weidema, 2000). For example, a novel, currently underexploited, source for organic fertilizers is dried microbial biomass produced when treating industrial wastewaters with low levels of undesired contaminants (e.g. heavy metals), for instance from the food and beverage industry (Spa- noghe et al., 2020). The high nutrient concentrations in these waste- waters (up to 3000 mg/L of N and 600 mg/L of P) present a resource recovery opportunity, not only for N, P, and K, but also for C (Falletti et al., 2014). Like most organic fertilizers, microbial fertilizers can contribute to replenish the organic matter content in the soil (Sedlar et al., 2023) and allow a slow nutrient release, potentially avoiding nutrient losses (Shaji et al., 2021). Microbial fertilizers have also been shown to improve crop quality (Coppens et al., 2016) and health (Wambacq et al., 2022), compared to other organic fertilizers.

P, and K to ensure a fair comparison. As there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, the second objective was to assess the contribution of the different organic fertilizers to the achievement of the goal of the "4 per 1000" Initiative. As there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, the second objective was to assess the contribution of the different organic fertilizers to the achievement of the goal of the "4 per 1000" Initiative. This paper will both analyse the environmental impact of fertilizers and also inform LCA practitioners about the intricacies of using the consequential Ecoinvent database to assess fertilizer products.

of user expectations, efficiency, complex supply chains, and scaling is- sues (Spiller et al., 2024).

If the demand for organic fertilizers will increase in the future, it is important to understand the environmental impacts of these fertilizers to avoid unexpected consequences. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable and widely accepted method for the evaluation of these im- pacts. LCA studies comparing organic to conventional agriculture including but not limited to fertilizer choice - often show a better environmental performance for organic products per unit of land occupied, but a worse performance per unit of product (due to lower yields) (Meier et al., 2015). To the knowledge of the authors, there are only a few comparative assessments of major groups of organic fertil- izers (Avadí, 2020; Litskas, 2023), and comparability between LCA studies is highly limited due to different assumptions and methods applied. For instance, the functional unit (FU) of LCA studies on bio-based fertilizers often refer to the mass of nutrients (N or P), or total mass/volume of the final product (Egas et al., 2023). Since nutrient ratios vary between organic fertilizers, such a FU does not reflect the fertilizers' actual functionality. Moreover, most available LCAs comparing organic and conventional agricultural products apply an attributional modelling approach (Meier et al., 2015) to study the environmental impact of an average product. Instead, consequential modelling is more appropriate when investigating the environmental consequences of a change in the system, and it is the recommended method when using LCA for policy-making or strategic environmental planning (Weidema et al., 2018).

Consequential modelling also attempts to reflect the economic re- alities behind the supply and demand of goods. Commonly used organic fertilizers are often constrained by resource availability. Manures, for example, are low-priced by-products produced along with a determining product (e.g. meat or milk). In the case of an increased demand for manure, the farmers will not be able to change their production vol- umes. The new suppliers that will absorb this demand are those that can still increase their production capacity for a functionally similar prod- uct, or marginal suppliers. In the case of fertilizers, mineral fertilizer producers are the marginal suppliers. Hence, demanding an extra unit of manure will have the same environmental impact as demanding an extra unit of mineral fertilizer (Fig. 1).

An exception to the constrained nature of by-products occurs when these are not fully utilized (i.e. unconstrained). In that case, the demand for an additional unit can be satisfied by untapped by-products, and the waste management company becomes the marginal supplier (Weidema, 2000). For example, a novel, currently underexploited, source for organic fertilizers is dried microbial biomass produced when treating industrial wastewaters with low levels of undesired contaminants (e.g. heavy metals), for instance from the food and beverage industry (Spa- noghe et al., 2020). The high nutrient concentrations in these waste- waters (up to 3000 mg/L of N and 600 mg/L of P) present a resource recovery opportunity, not only for N, P, and K, but also for C (Falletti et al., 2014). Microbial fertilizer types that have been considered are based on Aerobic Heterotrophic Bacteria (AHB), Microalgae and AHB (MaB), and Purple Non-Sulphur Bacteria (PNSB) - AHB and MaB pro- duction technologies are commercially available, although the micro- bial biomass is currently not valorised as fertilizer, while PNSB is currently produced in pilot scale (Spiller et al., 2020). Like most organic fertilizers, microbial fertilizers can contribute to replenish the organic matter content in the soil (Sedlar et al., 2023) and allow a slow nutrient release, potentially avoiding nutrient losses (Shaji et al., 2021). Microbial fertilizers have also been shown to improve crop quality (Coppens et al., 2016) and health (Wambacq et al., 2022), compared to other organic fertilizers.

This study aims to quantify the environmental impact of replacing mineral NPK fertilizers with 14 conventional and 3 innovative organic fertilizers, accounting for market constraints through consequential LCA. A comparative assessment of this extent had never been carried out before for fertilizer products. The chosen multi-substance FU includes N,

P, and K to ensure a fair comparison. As there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, the second objective was to assess the contribution of the different organic fertilizers to the achievement of the goal of the "4 per 1000" Initiative. This paper will both analyse the environmental impact of fertilizers and also inform LCA practitioners about the intricacies of using the consequential Ecoinvent database to assess fertilizer products.
21: The FU of the LCA is a compound fertilizer, containing 1 t of nitro- gen, 0.44 t of phosphorus, and 0.83 of potassium. These amounts relate to NPK 15-15-15, one of the most common compound fertilizers, con- sisting of the same mass of N, phosphorus pentoxide (1 of P2O5 = 0.44 t of P), and potassium oxide (1 ton of K2O = 0.83 t of K) (European Commission, 2019). As the release properties of nutrients vary between different fertilizers, the mineral fertilizer equivalent (MFE) was considered for all organic fertilizers. Thereby, a functional equivalence is ensured. The MFE for N (NMFE) was taken from literature whenever data was available or, when this was not the case, it was calculated following a linear relationship between the C/N ratio and NMFE described by Delin et al. (2012) (Table 1). P availability is highly dependent on soil pH (Delin, 2016) and, consequently, comparing phosphorus MFEs (PMFEs) taken from different studies would not be consistent. Therefore, an estimative value of 50% (from Delin's two-month experimental study analysing the PMFE of different organic residues) was assumed for all the organic fertilizers (Delin, 2016). It is important to note that other authors have measured PMFEs of up to 100% for organic fertilizers (Christiansen et al., 2020; Vanden Nest et al., 2021), so the P content of these organic materials might be underestimated in the present study. Potassium present in organic ma- terials is highly soluble in water and readily available for plants (Abd-Elrahman and Taha, 2018). For this reason, all organic fertilizers were assumed to have a KMFE of 100%.

Organic fertilizers differ in their nutrient ratios. To enable their comparison, the FU was assembled by adding organic fertilizer such that the amount of NPK did not exceed the amounts defined above, and the shortage was supplemented with mineral fertilizers. Specifically, this was implemented by first classifying products into N, P, or K fertilizer. This was done by calculating the reference flow needed for each fertil- izer to reach the FU for N, P, and K, always taking into account the MFE (Table 1). The fertilizer was then classified into the nutrient category with the lowest needed reference flow. For example, as solid cattle manure needs 350 t to reach the FU for N, 635 t to reach the FU for P, and 138 t to reach the FU for K, it was classified as a K fertilizer. The reference flow for this fertilizer is thus 138 t, containing 0.39 t of N, 0.10 t of P, and 0.83 t of K (all readily available). After that, the FU for the three nutrients was fulfilled by adding to the organic fertilizer reference flow the rest of the other two nutrients in the form of straight (i.e. single- nutrient) mineral fertilizer specifically, urea (in this case 0.61 single superphosphate (0.34 and/or potassium chloride (0.00 Details for all fertilizers can be found in Table 1.

The three microbial fertilizers (AHB, MaB, and PNSB) are cultivated on wastewater from the potato industry in Belgium. They were therefore modelled considering Belgian foreground inventory data, marginal technologies, and legislation. The process descriptions, parameters, as well as the and outputs of materials, energy, and emissions for each of the microbial fertilizer production systems and the baseline system were taken from Spiller et al. (2020). The Excel model of Spiller et al. (2020) was revised to include new data and insights, including an update of the PNSB system to replace the photobioreactor with a more economical and energy-efficient raceway reactor (Alloul et al., 2021) (Appendix A, Figure a).
All the material and energy flows needed to produce the microbial

fertilizers, as well as the emissions resulting from these processes, were introduced in the Activity Browser 2.7.4 software, with data from the Ecoinvent 3.9.1 database (consequential system model). The environ- mental impacts of these microbial fertilizers were compared to other organic fertilizers from animal (in particular: solid cattle manure, dried poultry manure, fishmeal, meat and bone meal, horn meal, dried sewage sludge from municipal wastewater treatment - mechanical dewatering followed by thermal drying swine slurry, and cattle slurry) and plant origin (rapeseed meal, soybean meal, green manure, compost, sugarcane vinasse, sugar beet molasse), as well as to a blend of the most consumed single-nutrient mineral fertilizers: urea, single superphosphate, and potassium chloride (Nadarajan and Sukumaran, 2021). Combinations of organic and mineral fertilizers were thus compared to a purely mineral one with the same nutrient concentrations. Inventory data for these processes was taken from Ecoinvent. The processes categorized as con- strained in the database were modified to adapt the nutrient supply flows (linked to mineral fertilizers) to the nutrient contents shown in Table 1. This study includes the life stages from the extraction of raw materials until the production of the fertilizer. The use stage (land application), needed infrastructure, and the product packaging are excluded from the assessment. The results were calculated using the ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assessment method (hierarchist, with global normalization), and presented in three endpoint impact cate- gories: human health (measured in disability-adjusted life years DALYs), ecosystem quality (measured in local species loss integrated over time, species.year), and resource depletion (measured in USD, 2013). The reason for choosing endpoint indicators is that they reflect the damage to human health, natural resources, or biodiversity, while also allowing for easier interpretation of the results through aggregation (RIVM, 2011). Monte Carlo simulations were implemented in the Ac- tivity Browser with 1000 iterations to estimate the uncertainty of the results.

Since most unconstrained organic fertilizers showed a relatively big impact coming from heat consumption, two different scenarios were calculated: one including heat processes, and a second one assuming that the unconstrained fertilizers are produced in an industrial symbiosis system with waste heat from another operation. The activities were modified to exclude heat processes in the foreground system. A third scenario with extended system boundaries was calculated to estimate the impact of transporting mineral fertilizers from supplying countries to Europe (Belgium was chosen as the target country) - as one of the motivations for the application of organic fertilizers is that they could be produced/recovered locally, reducing the dependency on imported nu- trients. The average distance travelled by imported fertilizers was calculated taking into account import shares (Fertilizers Europe) and multiplied by the weight of the mineral fertilizer needed to reach the FU (Appendix A, Equation a). The freight transport was added to the min- eral fertilizer model. In a further step, the maximum distance that the organic fertilizers could be transported to perform better than the mineral one in all categories was calculated (Appendix A, Equation b).

To enable their comparison, the FU was assembled by adding organic fertilizer such that the amount of NPK did not exceed the amounts defined above, and the shortage was supplemented with mineral fertilizers. Specifically, this was implemented by first classifying products into N, P, or K fertilizer. This was done by calculating the reference flow needed for each fertil- izer to reach the FU for N, P, and K, always taking into account the MFE (Table 1). The fertilizer was then classified into the nutrient category with the lowest needed reference flow. For example, as solid cattle manure needs 350 t to reach the FU for N, 635 t to reach the FU for P, and 138 t to reach the FU for K, it was classified as a K fertilizer. The reference flow for this fertilizer is thus 138 t, containing 0.39 t of N, 0.10 t of P, and 0.83 t of K (all readily available). After that, the FU for the three nutrients was fulfilled by adding to the organic fertilizer reference flow the rest of the other two nutrients in the form of straight (i.e. single- nutrient) mineral fertilizer specifically, urea (in this case 0.61 single superphosphate (0.34 and/or potassium chloride (0.00 Details for all fertilizers can be found in Table 1.

The three microbial fertilizers (AHB, MaB, and PNSB) are cultivated on wastewater from the potato industry in Belgium. They were therefore modelled considering Belgian foreground inventory data, marginal technologies, and legislation. The process descriptions, parameters, as well as the and outputs of materials, energy, and emissions for each of the microbial fertilizer production systems and the baseline system were taken from Spiller et al. (2020). The Excel model of Spiller et al. (2020) was revised to include new data and insights, including an update of the PNSB system to replace the photobioreactor with a more economical and energy-efficient raceway reactor (Alloul et al., 2021) (Appendix A, Figure a).
All the material and energy flows needed to produce the microbial

fertilizers, as well as the emissions resulting from these processes, were introduced in the Activity Browser 2.7.4 software, with data from the Ecoinvent 3.9.1 database (consequential system model). The environ- mental impacts of these microbial fertilizers were compared to other organic fertilizers from animal (in particular: solid cattle manure, dried poultry manure, fishmeal, meat and bone meal, horn meal, dried sewage sludge from municipal wastewater treatment - mechanical dewatering followed by thermal drying swine slurry, and cattle slurry) and plant origin (rapeseed meal, soybean meal, green manure, compost, sugarcane vinasse, sugar beet molasse), as well as to a blend of the most consumed single-nutrient mineral fertilizers: urea, single superphosphate, and potassium chloride (Nadarajan and Sukumaran, 2021). Combinations of organic and mineral fertilizers were thus compared to a purely mineral one with the same nutrient concentrations. Inventory data for these processes was taken from Ecoinvent. The processes categorized as con- strained in the database were modified to adapt the nutrient supply flows (linked to mineral fertilizers) to the nutrient contents shown in Table 1. This study includes the life stages from the extraction of raw materials until the production of the fertilizer. The use stage (land application), needed infrastructure, and the product packaging are excluded from the assessment. The results were calculated using the ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assessment method (hierarchist, with global normalization), and presented in three endpoint impact cate- gories: human health (measured in disability-adjusted life years DALYs), ecosystem quality (measured in local species loss integrated over time, species.year), and resource depletion (measured in USD, 2013). The reason for choosing endpoint indicators is that they reflect the damage to human health, natural resources, or biodiversity, while also allowing for easier interpretation of the results through aggregation (RIVM, 2011). Monte Carlo simulations were implemented in the Ac- tivity Browser with 1000 iterations to estimate the uncertainty of the results.

Since most unconstrained organic fertilizers showed a relatively big impact coming from heat consumption, two different scenarios were calculated: one including heat processes, and a second one assuming that the unconstrained fertilizers are produced in an industrial symbiosis system with waste heat from another operation. The activities were modified to exclude heat processes in the foreground system. A third scenario with extended system boundaries was calculated to estimate the impact of transporting mineral fertilizers from supplying countries to Europe (Belgium was chosen as the target country) - as one of the motivations for the application of organic fertilizers is that they could be produced/recovered locally, reducing the dependency on imported nu- trients. The average distance travelled by imported fertilizers was calculated taking into account import shares (Fertilizers Europe) and multiplied by the weight of the mineral fertilizer needed to reach the FU (Appendix A, Equation a). The freight transport was added to the min- eral fertilizer model. In a further step, the maximum distance that the organic fertilizers could be transported to perform better than the mineral one in all categories was calculated (Appendix A, Equation b).
26: The FU of the LCA is a compound fertilizer, containing 1 t of nitro- gen, 0.44 t of phosphorus, and 0.83 of potassium. These amounts relate to NPK 15-15-15, one of the most common compound fertilizers, con- sisting of the same mass of N, phosphorus pentoxide (1 of P2O5 = 0.44 t of P), and potassium oxide (1 ton of K2O = 0.83 t of K) (European Commission, 2019). As the release properties of nutrients vary between different fertilizers, the mineral fertilizer equivalent (MFE) was considered for all organic fertilizers. Thereby, a functional equivalence is ensured. The MFE for N (NMFE) was taken from literature whenever data was available or, when this was not the case, it was calculated following a linear relationship between the C/N ratio and NMFE described by Delin et al. (2012) (Table 1). P availability is highly dependent on soil pH (Delin, 2016) and, consequently, comparing phosphorus MFEs (PMFEs) taken from different studies would not be consistent. Therefore, an estimative value of 50% (from Delin's two-month experimental study analysing the PMFE of different organic residues) was assumed for all the organic fertilizers (Delin, 2016). It is important to note that other authors have measured PMFEs of up to 100% for organic fertilizers (Christiansen et al., 2020; Vanden Nest et al., 2021), so the P content of these organic materials might be underestimated in the present study. Potassium present in organic ma- terials is highly soluble in water and readily available for plants (Abd-Elrahman and Taha, 2018). For this reason, all organic fertilizers were assumed to have a KMFE of 100%.

27: A specific methodological aspect of consequential modelling lies in the way in which by-products are treated. The ISO 14044 standards suggest avoiding allocation wherever possible and instead applying system expansion (2006b). This is done differently for by-products that are fully utilized than for those not fully utilized. When a by-product is constrained, the demand for an extra unit will increase the production of a functionally similar product supplied by the marginal supplier (Fig. 1, left). When a by-product is not constrained, an additional demand will only result in the displacement of waste processing (Fig. 1, right). (Weidema and Schmidt, 2022).

The fertilizers were classified into determining products or by- products, and by-products were further categorized into constrained or unconstrained, following their description in the Ecoinvent 3.9.1

database. In the case of constrained by-products, the marginal supplier in the database was assumed to be correct. Microbial fertilizers were considered unconstrained by-products because of the high nutrient concentrations in wastewaters from the food and beverage industry that are currently untapped (see introduction).
28: To determine the contribution of the organic fertilizers to the "4 per 1000" initiative's goal, the C content (C content) of each fertilizer was obtained from literature and multiplied by the reference flow (Refflow). The effective C sequestration (Cseq) was assumed to be 2-14%, based on previous studies that estimated C still bound to soil after 100 years (European Commission, 2001; Hansen et al., 2006; Favoino and Hogg, 2008; Boldrin et al., 2009). Since the amount of C applied is limited by the N application (Napplication), two scenarios were calculated: one sce- nario for high N application (as practiced in Flanders, BE), in which 220 kg N/ha/year are applied on average (VLM, 2021), and a lower appli- cation scenario that reflects the global average N application of 75 kg N/ha/year (FAO, 2023; FAO, 2020a). In Flanders, the FU, containing one ton of N (FUN), could be used to fertilize less than 5 ha, while at a global average N application, it could be used to fertilize more than 13 ha. Consequently, the C application and resulting increase of SOC differs as a function of the N and C content of the fertilizers and the assumed application rate.
Equation 1Calculation of SOC increase by application of organic fertilizers.

30: Results show that 9 out of 17 organic fertilizers (manures, meat and bone meal, rapeseed meal, compost, vinasse, and molasse) are defined as constrained by-products in the Ecoinvent database and their impact is therefore related to a marginal supplier (Table 1). Instead, all the mi- crobial fertilizers, horn meal, and sewage sludge are classified as un- constrained by-products. Fish meal, soybean meal, and green manure are determining products manufactured for their function as feed (fish- and soybean meal) or fertilizer (green manure).
31: LCA results are shown in Fig. 2 as relative impact, with the most impactful fertilizer in each category representing 100%.

For human health (HH), rapeseed meal (100%), soybean meal (85%), and fishmeal (79%) show the highest impact. The first two are related to soybean meal directly (soybean meal fertilizer) or indirectly (constrained product in the protein feed market). Horn meal (5%), mineral fertilizer (8%), and sewage sludge (1%) show the lowest impact. These fertilizers are all unconstrained. The results for microbial fertilizer vary between 42% for AHB and 52% for MaB. Animal-based fertilizers show an impact ranging between 5% (horn meal), and 79% (fishmeal). Plant-based fertilizers have an impact between 11% (sugarcane vinasse) and 100% (rapeseed meal).
In the category of ecosystem quality (EQ), the highest impact is

Prices of soybean oil and meal rise in low production periods in key regions like Argentina, Brazil, and USA, and decrease with global stock increase (López, 2021). For this study, soybean meal is considered a determining product.

found for green manure (100%), followed by rapeseed meal (19%), and soybean meal (18%). In this category too, some of the highest impacts are directly or indirectly related to soybean meal (except in the case of green manure). The impact of the other fertilizers is relatively low, with 7% for meat and bone meal, 4% for sugar beet molasse, 3% for MaB, 2% for fishmeal, and 1% or less for all the rest.

The results for the resource depletion (RD) category show the highest impact for AHB (100%) followed by PNSB (88%), and rapeseed meal (31%). MaB has the lowest impact (-15%) followed by horn meal (7%), and dried sewage sludge (7%). Mineral fertilizers show an impact of 8% in this category.

Constrained fertilizers linked to mineral fertilizer (solid cattle manure, dried poultry manure, swine slurry, cattle slurry, compost, and sugarcane vinasse) have an impact of around 13% for the category HH, 1% for EQ, and 10% for RD. Small fluctuations in comparison with the results for mineral fertilizer are caused by the difference between the Ecoinvent mineral fertilizer processes they are linked to. The mineral fertilizers used to supplement the organic products to reach the FU are linked to European markets for single-nutrient fertilizers (urea, single superphosphate, and potassium chloride), while the Ecoinvent processes for constrained organic fertilizers are linked to global markets for N, P2O5, and K2O fertilizers.

The other constrained fertilizers (meat and bone meal, rapeseed meal, and sugar beet molasse) are linked to markets for protein feed (global, marginal supplier soybean meal) and energy feed (Europe, barley grain; South Africa, maize grain), in different ratios. Meat and bone meal and rapeseed meal are primarily linked to soybean meal, while sugar beet molasse is mainly linked to barley and maize. There- fore, the difference between soybean meal and these fertilizers can be more pronounced (up to 60% difference between soybean meal and sugar beet molasse in the category HH).

Detailed results of the Monte Carlo simulations can be found in Ap- pendix A, Figure e. Horn meal showed a lower impact than mineral fertilizers in 100% of the runs along all impact categories. AHB (53% of the runs) and PNSB (51%) showed a majority of better results than mineral fertilizers in the ecosystem quality category. In the resource depletion category, MaB and sewage sludge performed better than mineral fertilizers in 100% of the runs. The rest of the fertilizers showed worse results than mineral fertilizers in most of the runs.

Aerobic heterotrophic bacteria|| Microalgae and AHB|| Purple non-sulphur bacteria|| Solid cattle manure|| Dried poultry manure|| Fishmeal|| Meat and bone meal|| Horn meal|| Dried sewage sludge|| Swine slurry|| Cattle slurry|| Rapeseed meal|| Soybean meal|| Green manure|| Compost|| Sugarcane vinasse|| Sugar beet molasse|| Mineral fertilizer|| |0|25 50 75 100

Fig. 2. Comparative assessment of all analysed fertilizers in the three end-point impact categories. Grey backgrounds represent constrained products and white backgrounds unconstrained products. Abbreviations: AHB = aerobic heterotrophic bacteria; DALYs = disability adjusted life years.

found for green manure (100%), followed by rapeseed meal (19%), and soybean meal (18%). In this category too, some of the highest impacts are directly or indirectly related to soybean meal (except in the case of green manure). The impact of the other fertilizers is relatively low, with 7% for meat and bone meal, 4% for sugar beet molasse, 3% for MaB, 2% for fishmeal, and 1% or less for all the rest.

The results for the resource depletion (RD) category show the highest impact for AHB (100%) followed by PNSB (88%), and rapeseed meal (31%). MaB has the lowest impact (-15%) followed by horn meal (7%), and dried sewage sludge (7%). Mineral fertilizers show an impact of 8% in this category.

Constrained fertilizers linked to mineral fertilizer (solid cattle manure, dried poultry manure, swine slurry, cattle slurry, compost, and sugarcane vinasse) have an impact of around 13% for the category HH, 1% for EQ, and 10% for RD. Small fluctuations in comparison with the results for mineral fertilizer are caused by the difference between the Ecoinvent mineral fertilizer processes they are linked to. The mineral fertilizers used to supplement the organic products to reach the FU are linked to European markets for single-nutrient fertilizers (urea, single superphosphate, and potassium chloride), while the Ecoinvent processes for constrained organic fertilizers are linked to global markets for N, P2O5, and K2O fertilizers.

The other constrained fertilizers (meat and bone meal, rapeseed meal, and sugar beet molasse) are linked to markets for protein feed (global, marginal supplier soybean meal) and energy feed (Europe, barley grain; South Africa, maize grain), in different ratios. Meat and bone meal and rapeseed meal are primarily linked to soybean meal, while sugar beet molasse is mainly linked to barley and maize. There- fore, the difference between soybean meal and these fertilizers can be more pronounced (up to 60% difference between soybean meal and sugar beet molasse in the category HH).

Detailed results of the Monte Carlo simulations can be found in Ap- pendix A, Figure e. Horn meal showed a lower impact than mineral fertilizers in 100% of the runs along all impact categories. AHB (53% of the runs) and PNSB (51%) showed a majority of better results than mineral fertilizers in the ecosystem quality category. In the resource depletion category, MaB and sewage sludge performed better than mineral fertilizers in 100% of the runs. The rest of the fertilizers showed worse results than mineral fertilizers in most of the runs.
35: LCA results are shown in Fig. 2 as relative impact, with the most impactful fertilizer in each category representing 100%.

For human health (HH), rapeseed meal (100%), soybean meal (85%), and fishmeal (79%) show the highest impact. The first two are related to soybean meal directly (soybean meal fertilizer) or indirectly (constrained product in the protein feed market). Horn meal (5%), mineral fertilizer (8%), and sewage sludge (1%) show the lowest impact. These fertilizers are all unconstrained. The results for microbial fertilizer vary between 42% for AHB and 52% for MaB. Animal-based fertilizers show an impact ranging between 5% (horn meal), and 79% (fishmeal). Plant-based fertilizers have an impact between 11% (sugarcane vinasse) and 100% (rapeseed meal).
In the category of ecosystem quality (EQ), the highest impact is

For this study, soybean meal is considered a determining product.

37: All the manure-based fertilizers, compost, and vinasse only show impacts coming from mineral N, P, and K fertilizers and a small contribution from transport (Fig. 3). Since meat and bone meal, rape- seed, and sugar beet molasse are traded in the protein feed and energy feed markets, they show impacts coming from soybean (marginal sup- plier in the protein feed market), barley, and maize (marginal suppliers in the energy feed market). The contribution to the impact of mineral fertilizers and soybean meal are further described in upcoming sections.
38: The main process contributing to human health impacts is the avoided baseline for all microbial fertilizers (Fig. 3). Since the baseline system (UASB followed by initrification-denitrification) generates heat and electricity (Appendix A, Figure a), it avoids conventional fossil- based energy production, therefore avoiding CO2 emissions. Hence, replacing the baseline treatment with the production of microbial fer- tilizers has an impact of 3.59x10-2 DALYs for AHB, 4.03x10 DALYs

for MaB, and 8.80x10- DALYs for PNSB. These values differ between the microbial fertilizers due to the differences in the reference flow (MaB requires the highest amount of wastewater to reach the FU, and AHB the lowest). The production processes of MaB and PNSB also generate en- ergy (Appendix A, Figure a), avoiding an impact on human health through electricity (-1.42x10-1 DALYs for MaB and - 9.49x101 DALYs for PNSB) and heat (-1.20x10 DALYs for MaB and -3.53x10 DALYs for PNSB). On the contrary, AHB consumes energy, which leads to an impact of 1.43x10-2 DALYs from heat and 6.24x10 DALYs from electricity. Apart from the energy-related impacts, AHB and PNSB show a lower contribution from ammonium sulphate (1.44x10 and 1.29x10-2 DALYs, respectively), which is added to the wastewater to maximize biomass growth or avoid N limitation. MaB also presents an impact from direct emissions (1.37x10) DALYs) related to ammonia gas (NH3) originating during biomass cultivation in the raceway reactor.

For ecosystem quality, ammonium sulphate shows the highest contribution from the AHB production (2.51x10-5 species.year) and also has a high impact on the PNSB production (2.24 x101 species. year), mainly through direct emissions during the production of liquid ammonia (fossil CO2). Also in this impact category, avoiding the base- line wastewater treatment creates an impact, because the energy from biogas will need to be replaced by fossil fuels (1.57x10) species.year for AHB, 1.76 x10 species.year for MaB, and 3.83 x10 species.year for PNSB). As an electricity consumer, AHB has an impact of species.year, while the electricity producers show an avoided impact (-3.39x10) species.year for MaB and -2.27x10-5 species.year for PNSB). Heat in Europe is co-generated along with electricity in com- bined cycle power plants using natural gas. The alternative process to this type of electricity production would be the burning of wood chips. To produce that fuel, land needs to be transformed into forest, which has an impact on ecosystem quality. Due to this model, heat consumption (AHB) avoids an impact, while heat generation (MaB, PNSB) causes an impact. Direct emissions show an impact for MaB (4.11x10 species. year) and PNSB (2.32x10-5 species.year). Finally, AHB and PNSB show a small impact coming from triple superphosphate that is added to avoid P limitation.

The avoided baseline again shows a big impact on the resources category (4040 USD for AHB, 45,300 USD for MaB, and 9880 USD for PNSB). The impact comes from avoiding the production of energy from biogas (as a replacement for natural gas). AHB shows an impact for consuming natural gas for heat (4500 USD) and electricity (612 USD), while MaB (-37,400 USD for heat, -12,200 USD for electricity) and PNSB (-1100 USD for heat, -814 USD for electricity) avoid the con- sumption of this natural resource.

42: Also animal-based organic fertilizers mainly present impacts related to energy (Fig. 3). For human health, fishmeal (1.17x10) DALYs) and dried sewage sludge (7.89x10-3 DALYs) show major contributions from emissions to air (particulate matter, sulphur dioxide, carbon dioxide) originating from the lignite-based heat production. The drying of sewage sludge to use it as a fertilizer avoids the current waste treatment mix (60% drying and use as fertilizer or alternative fuel or raw material, 40% municipal incineration). Since the incineration creates heat that can be reused, avoiding this process creates an impact of 1.14x10-2 DALYs. Horn meal shows a relatively small contribution from electricity (1.15x10- DALYs) and an avoided impact from heat (-1.19x10-3 DALYs). This relates to the fact that the heat (RoW) is co-generated with electricity from natural gas, avoiding particulate matter emissions from lignite and wood-based electricity production.

In the ecosystem quality category, the biggest impact for fishmeal (1.39x10-4 species.year) again comes from emissions during heat pro- duction (fossil CO2, phosphate to groundwater, sulphur dioxide to air). Also sewage sludge again shows the main impact from the avoided (heat-producing) baseline (3.79x10-5 species.year), since other fuels, such as wood chips, would replace that heat (forest occupation needed).

Fig. 3. Process contributions to total impact in the categories of human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. Results are presented in percentages, to show the relative impact of each process. The dotted bars represent the impact of the single-nutrient fertilizer added to organic fertilizers to reach the functional unit. For constrained products, stripped bars represent the impact of functionally similar products supplied by the marginal supplier (indicated between brackets).

This impact is followed by that from heat (1,20x10 species.year Horn meal has a similar impact to that in the previous impact category too, showing a relatively small impact from heat (2.01x10 6 species. year) and electricity (1.94x10 species.year).

In the resource category, fishmeal shows the main impact originating from transport (555 USD). This contribution comes mostly from the diesel used as fuel by the fishing vessel, sourced from the distillation of heavy oil. Both horn meal (445 USD) and dried sewage sludge (164 USD) show a relatively big contribution from natural gas-based heat. Avoiding the municipal incineration of sewage sludge leads to an avoided impact (-339 USD).
43: Also animal-based organic fertilizers mainly present impacts related to energy (Fig. 3). For human health, fishmeal (1.17x10) DALYs) and dried sewage sludge (7.89x10-3 DALYs) show major contributions from emissions to air (particulate matter, sulphur dioxide, carbon dioxide) originating from the lignite-based heat production. The drying of sewage sludge to use it as a fertilizer avoids the current waste treatment mix (60% drying and use as fertilizer or alternative fuel or raw material, 40% municipal incineration). Since the incineration creates heat that can be reused, avoiding this process creates an impact of 1.14x10-2 DALYs. Horn meal shows a relatively small contribution from electricity (1.15x10- DALYs) and an avoided impact from heat (-1.19x10-3 DALYs). This relates to the fact that the heat (RoW) is co-generated with electricity from natural gas, avoiding particulate matter emissions from lignite and wood-based electricity production.

In the ecosystem quality category, the biggest impact for fishmeal (1.39x10-4 species.year) again comes from emissions during heat pro- duction (fossil CO2, phosphate to groundwater, sulphur dioxide to air). Also sewage sludge again shows the main impact from the avoided (heat-producing) baseline (3.79x10-5 species.year), since other fuels, such as wood chips, would replace that heat (forest occupation needed).

This impact is followed by that from heat (1,20x10 species.year Horn meal has a similar impact to that in the previous impact category too, showing a relatively small impact from heat (2.01x10 6 species. year) and electricity (1.94x10 species.year).

In the resource category, fishmeal shows the main impact originating from transport (555 USD). This contribution comes mostly from the diesel used as fuel by the fishing vessel, sourced from the distillation of heavy oil. Both horn meal (445 USD) and dried sewage sludge (164 USD) show a relatively big contribution from natural gas-based heat. Avoiding the municipal incineration of sewage sludge leads to an avoided impact (-339 USD).
48: Also animal-based organic fertilizers mainly present impacts related to energy (Fig. 3). For human health, fishmeal (1.17x10) DALYs) and dried sewage sludge (7.89x10-3 DALYs) show major contributions from emissions to air (particulate matter, sulphur dioxide, carbon dioxide) originating from the lignite-based heat production. The drying of sewage sludge to use it as a fertilizer avoids the current waste treatment mix (60% drying and use as fertilizer or alternative fuel or raw material, 40% municipal incineration). Since the incineration creates heat that can be reused, avoiding this process creates an impact of 1.14x10-2 DALYs. Horn meal shows a relatively small contribution from electricity (1.15x10- DALYs) and an avoided impact from heat (-1.19x10-3 DALYs). This relates to the fact that the heat (RoW) is co-generated with electricity from natural gas, avoiding particulate matter emissions from lignite and wood-based electricity production.

In the ecosystem quality category, the biggest impact for fishmeal (1.39x10-4 species.year) again comes from emissions during heat pro- duction (fossil CO2, phosphate to groundwater, sulphur dioxide to air). Also sewage sludge again shows the main impact from the avoided (heat-producing) baseline (3.79x10-5 species.year), since other fuels, such as wood chips, would replace that heat (forest occupation needed).

49: In the human health category, the main impact of soybean meal comes from the production of soybean (1.54x10 DALYs), which in- volves a land use change from forest to arable land, and emissions to air (Fig. 3). Soybean meal also shows an avoided impact (-3.36x10 DALYs) coming from the production of soybean oil as a by-product, which avoids the production of palm oil (and its emissions). For green manure, the main impact (6.60x10 DALYs) comes from direct nitro- gen oxide (N20) emissions during the cultivation, followed by emissions to air during mulching, sowing, and tillage (3.58x10 DALYs), and emissions to soil and air during rape seed production (2.04x10 2 DALYs).
For ecosystem quality, soybean meal shows the main contribution

coming from the land occupation for soybean production (1.92x10-3 species.year), as well as a small avoided impact from soybean oil (-1.11x10-4 species.year). Almost the whole impact of green manure (9.65x10-3 species.year) is related to the occupation of land.

Regarding natural resources, soybean meal shows a contribution from soybean production (855 USD), transport (531 USD), heat (528 USD), soybean oil (353 USD), and electricity (58 USD), all contributing mainly to the depletion of natural gas. Green manure shows the major impact coming from oil and gas consumed by agricultural machinery during tillage, mulching, and sowing (1311 USD), followed by the nat- ural gas used to dry the seeds (112 USD).

51: The biggest contribution to the impact of reaching the functional unit only by using single-nutrient mineral fertilizers for the category human health comes from the sulphur dioxide emissions released to the air during the production of sulfuric acid (6.65x10 DALYs), which is used together with phosphate rock to produce single superphosphate. Urea has the second biggest contribution due to emissions to air during the ammonia production process (5.40x10 DALYs). The impact of potas- sium chloride (2.36x10-3 DALYs) is mainly related to the heat needed to dissolve the salts contained in the raw ore.

In the ecosystem quality category, urea shows the largest contribu- tion (1.22x10-5 species.year), since high amounts of fossil CO2 are emitted during ammonia production. Single superphosphate has a similar contribution (1.18x10 species.year related to sulphur dioxide and fossil CO2 emissions. Potassium chloride shows a contribution of 6.56x10 species.year, coming from CO2 emissions during heat production.

For the resource category, urea shows the main impact (552 USD), linked mainly to large amounts of natural gas used as hydrogen and energy source for ammonia production. This is followed by potassium chloride (140 USD), which requires steam (and therefore natural gas) for its production. The impact of single superphosphate (88 USD) comes mainly from the depletion of phosphate rock.

53: Most of the unconstrained organic fertilizers show a major impact originating from heat consumption. If waste heat from nearby com- panies was used instead of fossil-based heat, the impact of some of these fertilizers would decrease (Appendix A, Figure c). For human health, the decrease would be 31% for AHB, 23% for PNSB, 71% for fishmeal, 159% for sewage sludge, and 7% for green manure. In the case of MaB, the net heat consumption is close to zero (Appendix A, Figure b), so there is no difference in this scenario. For ecosystem quality, fishmeal and sewage sludge show the highest relative changes (99% and 136% decrease, respectively). Regarding resources, AHB shows a decrease of 81%, MaB of 9%, PNSB of 83%, horn meal of 64%, sewage sludge of 69%, and soybean meal of 22%. The processes for green manure and mineral fertilizer don't include heat in the foreground and therefore stay unchanged.
54: Considering the amount of mineral fertilizer imported to the EU from other countries - mainly the UK, Ukraine, Norway, and Canada - (Fer- tilizers Europe), and taking Belgium as the target country, the average distance travelled by imported fertilizers amounts to 1984 km (Geo- datos, 2023) (Appendix A, Table b). If this distance is multiplied by the weight of the mineral fertilizer's FU (8.58 t) the necessary freight transport of 17,011.20 tkm is obtained. By adding this transport to the LCA, the impact of the mineral fertilizer could reach up to a maximum of 9.15x101 DALYs for human health (41% increase), 1.87x101 species. year for ecosystem quality (2% increase), and 5293 USD (47% increase) for resources (Appendix A, Figure d). Although the impact of all

fertilizers increases with the transportation of the mineral fertilizer needed to reach the FU, in the scenario with the most impactful trans- port option, many organic fertilizers (7 out of 17 for human health, 10 for ecosystem quality, and 15 for resource depletion) become a better option than the mineral one. In that scenario, transporting horn meal (2981 km), dried poultry manure (2457 km), swine slurry (221 km), solid cattle manure (151 km), compost (146 km), cattle slurry (78 km), and sugarcane vinasse (22 km) would still result in an advantage over choosing mineral fertilizer in all impact categories (Appendix, Figure f). In the scenario with the least impactful transport option, the additional impact from transportation is negligible (Appendix A, Figure d).

56: The "4 per 1000" Initiative's goal to sequester 0.6 tonnes of C per hectare per year is only achieved by rapeseed meal in the Flemish sce- nario (Fig. 4). This is linked to the high C content in the fertilizer and the low nutrient concentrations, which lead to a relatively high reference flow (amount of fertilizer that has to be added to reach the FU) (Ap- pendix A, Table a). In general, plant-based fertilizers have a higher C accumulation potential, as can be seen for rapeseed meal (max. in Flanders 0.72 t/ha/y), green manure (max. 0.57 t/ha/y), and soybean meal (max. 0.48 t/ha/y). The plant-based fertilizer with the lowest contribution is sugarcane vinasse (max. 0.38 t/ha/y). From the animal- based fertilizers, solid cattle manure (max. 0.42 t/ha/y) and dried poultry manure (max. 0.40 t/ha/y) hold the highest SOC increase poten- tial, while swine slurry only contributes to a max. of 0.16 t/ha/y. The microbial fertilizers all have a similar contribution (max. 0.25-0.27 t/ ha/y). From the unconstrained fertilizers, green manure, soybean meal, horn meal, and dried sewage sludge are those with the highest contri- bution to the achievement of the goal.
57: The consequential LCA as a modelling approach is not only inter- esting for comparing the environmental impact of different product systems, but it is also a way of thinking about how long we can maintain our current consumption patterns. Most of the fertilizers compared in this study are constrained, which means that it is currently impossible to increase their demand in the long term. The reasons that make a market constrained can vary and include the supply of only by-products that are fully utilized (constrained by the demand of determining products), shortages in the supply of raw materials (resource constraints), and regulations (political constraints). Since these aspects change with time and geographical location, constrained markets can also shift.

In this study, the microbial fertilizers are considered to be uncon- strained, because Belgian potato wastewater treatment plants usually remove N (activated sludge process is applied with nitrification- denitrification, releasing N2 to the air) and P (chemical precipitation with FeCl3 dosing, followed by sludge landfilling or incineration), making these nutrients an untapped resource (Spiller et al., 2020). More than 6.2 Mt of potatoes are processed yearly in Belgium (VILT, 2023), consuming between 1.25 and 5 m³ of water per ton of potato (Van den Abeele et al., 2015). This means that around 20 million m³ of waste- water, containing 5.81 Gg of N and 1.16 Gg of P, need to be treated each year. These nutrients have the potential to replace 7% of the N (worth over 7 million euros) and 2% of the P (worth over 4 million euros) applied as mineral fertilizers in Flanders (total yearly application rates taken from Vingerhoets et al. (2023), fertilizer prices taken from Quinn (2023)). Considering this, recovering these nutrients should be priori- tized over removing them (although for this transition several chal- lenges related to product variability, complex supply chains, and scale need to be addressed). The environmental impact of these innovative fertilizers could still be reduced, for example in an industrial symbiosis

Fig. 4. Contribution to the achievement of the "4 per 1000" Initiative through the application of organic fertilizers. The minimum is related to a Cseq of 2%, and the maximum to a Cseq of 14% (after 100 years). Abbreviations: AHB = aerobic heterotrophic bacteria; MaB = microalgae and AHB consortium; PNSB = purple non- sulphur bacteria.

For the rest of the compared fertilizers, the (un-)constrained nature of the products was adopted from the Ecoinvent 3.9.1 database. Although this study provides an indication for practitioners performing consequential LCAs of organic fertilizers, this classification defined by Ecoinvent may be challenged, and constrained markets might differ between regions. For instance, all the animal manures are considered constrained in Ecoinvent because they are by-products from meat or dairy production that are fully utilized. However, in livestock-intensive regions, more manure is produced than what can be applied in the field (in nitrate-vulnerable zones there is a legal limit of 170 kg N/ha/y from livestock manure). For this reason, many of these regions export their manure (e.g. in Flanders 17.4% of the N and 37.7% of the P contained in livestock manure are exported) (Vingerhoets et al., 2023). Hence, these manures are locally unconstrained. Further research might compare the impact of using these organic fertilizers in constrained and uncon- strained scenarios. One suggestion to model an unconstrained manure market in consequential LCA (it is currently not available in Ecoinvent) would be to take the foreground data of a manure market from an attributional database and introduce it to a new process in the conse- quential database, maintaining the flows but linking to the corre- sponding background processes modelled consequentially.

Fishmeal is unconstrained according to Ecoinvent. Although the global fish market volume is increasing, with a record production of 178 million tonnes in 2020, stocks continue to decline due to overfishing (57.3% of marine fish stocks are fully exploited and 35.5% are overf- ished) and the share of captured fish used to produce fishmeal is declining (currently around 20%) (FAO, 2022b). The supply of fishmeal is lower than the demand, and the increasing price is driving some countries in West Africa to redirect fish that was historically used for human consumption to fishmeal production for export, putting food security at risk (FAO, 2022b). Taking these market trends into account, fishmeal should be considered a constrained market, because suppliers are no longer able to react to an increase in demand.

A similar case of resource constraint is that of soybean meal. Global soy production has exploded over the past 50 years partly thanks to increasing crop yields (150% increase), but mostly through a land area expansion of 300% (Ritchie et al., 2021). The main producers are the US, Brazil, and Argentina, and 77% of soy is used to feed livestock (only 7% is fed directly as soybeans, but the rest is first processed into soybean meal) (Fraanje and Garnett, 2020). The land use change from natural forests and less protected ecosystems, such as grasslands, to croplands contributes to climate change (Castanheira and Freire, 2013) and biodiversity loss (Chaudhary and Kastner, 2016; Säumel et al., 2023).

Since the market for soybean meal is still increasing, it cannot be considered a constrained market yet, but it has been recommended to limit the use of soy as animal feed (Gebhardt et al., 2022). Following this recommendation, using soybean meal as fertilizer does not seem to be a coherent decision.

The European Commission's Farm to Fork Strategy and Circular Economy Action Plan, both building blocks of the European Green Deal, contain goals related to N, P, and soil organic C. These include the development of an Integrated Nutrient Management Plan to ensure a more sustainable nutrient application and stimulate the markets for recovered nutrients, the improvement of soil fertility, the reduction of nutrient losses by at least 50%, and the reduction of fertilizer use by at least 20% by 2030. Using organic fertilizers is one of the mentioned means to achieve these goals. However, region and time-specific knowledge of untapped nutrient flows is needed to plan a transition that has real potential to achieve those goals without burden-shifting. Hamelin et al. (2019), for example, applied a spatial approach to quantify the residual biomass potential in Europe, including residues from agriculture, forestry, urban greenery management, and food waste. Applying organic fertilizers that are constrained can contribute to a "greener" image but does not lead to the needed systematic change.

One suggestion to model an unconstrained manure market in consequential LCA (it is currently not available in Ecoinvent) would be to take the foreground data of a manure market from an attributional database and introduce it to a new process in the conse- quential database, maintaining the flows but linking to the corre- sponding background processes modelled consequentially.

Although the global fish market volume is increasing, with a record production of 178 million tonnes in 2020, stocks continue to decline due to overfishing (57.3% of marine fish stocks are fully exploited and 35.5% are overf- ished) and the share of captured fish used to produce fishmeal is declining (currently around 20%) (FAO, 2022b). Taking these market trends into account, fishmeal should be considered a constrained market, because suppliers are no longer able to react to an increase in demand.

A similar case of resource constraint is that of soybean meal. Global soy production has exploded over the past 50 years partly thanks to increasing crop yields (150% increase), but mostly through a land area expansion of 300% (Ritchie et al., 2021). The main producers are the US, Brazil, and Argentina, and 77% of soy is used to feed livestock (only 7% is fed directly as soybeans, but the rest is first processed into soybean meal) (Fraanje and Garnett, 2020). Since the market for soybean meal is still increasing, it cannot be considered a constrained market yet, but it has been recommended to limit the use of soy as animal feed (Gebhardt et al., 2022). Following this recommendation, using soybean meal as fertilizer does not seem to be a coherent decision.

The European Commission's Farm to Fork Strategy and Circular Economy Action Plan, both building blocks of the European Green Deal, contain goals related to N, P, and soil organic C. (2019), for example, applied a spatial approach to quantify the residual biomass potential in Europe, including residues from agriculture, forestry, urban greenery management, and food waste. 59: The consequential LCA as a modelling approach is not only inter- esting for comparing the environmental impact of different product systems, but it is also a way of thinking about how long we can maintain our current consumption patterns. Most of the fertilizers compared in this study are constrained, which means that it is currently impossible to increase their demand in the long term. The reasons that make a market constrained can vary and include the supply of only by-products that are fully utilized (constrained by the demand of determining products), shortages in the supply of raw materials (resource constraints), and regulations (political constraints). Since these aspects change with time and geographical location, constrained markets can also shift.

In this study, the microbial fertilizers are considered to be uncon- strained, because Belgian potato wastewater treatment plants usually remove N (activated sludge process is applied with nitrification- denitrification, releasing N2 to the air) and P (chemical precipitation with FeCl3 dosing, followed by sludge landfilling or incineration), making these nutrients an untapped resource (Spiller et al., 2020). More than 6.2 Mt of potatoes are processed yearly in Belgium (VILT, 2023), consuming between 1.25 and 5 m³ of water per ton of potato (Van den Abeele et al., 2015). This means that around 20 million m³ of waste- water, containing 5.81 Gg of N and 1.16 Gg of P, need to be treated each year. These nutrients have the potential to replace 7% of the N (worth over 7 million euros) and 2% of the P (worth over 4 million euros) applied as mineral fertilizers in Flanders (total yearly application rates taken from Vingerhoets et al. (2023), fertilizer prices taken from Quinn (2023)). Considering this, recovering these nutrients should be priori- tized over removing them (although for this transition several chal- lenges related to product variability, complex supply chains, and scale need to be addressed). The environmental impact of these innovative fertilizers could still be reduced, for example in an industrial symbiosis

The minimum is related to a Cseq of 2%, and the maximum to a Cseq of 14% (after 100 years). Abbreviations: AHB = aerobic heterotrophic bacteria; MaB = microalgae and AHB consortium; PNSB = purple non- sulphur bacteria.

For the rest of the compared fertilizers, the (un-)constrained nature of the products was adopted from the Ecoinvent 3.9.1 database.
