These include the development of an Integrated Nutrient Management Plan to ensure a more sustainable nutrient application and stimulate the markets for recovered nutrients, the improvement of soil fertility, the reduction of nutrient losses by at least 50%, and the reduction of fertilizer use by at least 20% by 2030. Using organic fertilizers is one of the mentioned means to achieve these goals. However, region and time-specific knowledge of untapped nutrient flows is needed to plan a transition that has real potential to achieve those goals without burden-shifting. Hamelin et al. (2019), for example, applied a spatial approach to quantify the residual biomass potential in Europe, including residues from agriculture, forestry, urban greenery management, and food waste. Applying organic fertilizers that are constrained can contribute to a "greener" image but does not lead to the needed systematic change.
64: The impact of the different fertilizers after field application was not included here due to data unavailability. A review article compared greenhouse gas emissions from mineral fertilizers, composts, digestates, and manures, and found that post-application methane (CH4) emissions are often higher when organic fertilizers are applied - especially in paddy fields and humid climates that exacerbate anaerobic conditions - (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). For nitrous oxide (N20), there is currently no consensus about organic fertilizers increasing or decreasing emissions compared to mineral fertilizers, since emission data varies depending on many factors, such as temperature, moisture content, oxygen availability, pH, and physicochemical properties of the organic fertilizers (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). These results match those of another review article on CH4 and N2O emissions in flooded rice systems (Linquist et al., 2012). The authors addressed the fact that there were variations between studies in how the manure is handled before application and indicated that composting and anaerobic digestion of manure or straw reduces CH4 emissions significantly (Chen et al., 2011; Corton et al., 2000). Regarding ammonia (NH3) emissions, two meta-analyses on Chinese croplands showed that the (full or partial) replacement of mineral fertilizers by manure significantly decreases NH3

emissions (Huang et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2022). Also in this case, factors such as land-spreading methods, meteorological parameters, soil mois- ture content, and type of manure have an impact on the emission rates (Wyer et al., 2022). However, anaerobically digesting manure/slurry generally increases the pH and could therefore lead to higher NH3 emissions (Nkoa, 2014; Pedersen and Hafner, 2023). Concerning nutrient losses to water, previous studies have shown that the applica- tion of manure, vermicompost, or biochar reduces N (nitrate and ammonium) and P (phosphate) leaching compared to mineral fertilizers (Yadav et al., 2019), and that digestates from slurry present a lower leaching potential than undigested slurry or mineral fertilizers (Walsh et al., 2012). Field experiments demonstrated that slurry application timing (higher nitrate leaching in autumn) also affects N leaching (Maris et al., 2021). LCA studies comparing organic to mineral fertilizers that include direct field emissions are scarce. Martínez-Blanco et al. (2009) compared the use of compost from organic municipal solid waste to the use of mineral fertilizer to grow tomatoes in Spain and found that the environmental impact of the compost scenario during the field opera- tions stage was lower for abiotic depletion, photochemical oxidation, and energy demand, but higher for global warming potential, acidifi- cation, and eutrophication. Martin et al. (2023) argues that applying human-urine-based fertilizers VS. mineral fertilizers can save impacts related to climate change, water use and eutrophication. An LCA comparing direct field application of untreated pig manure to a treat- ment scenario (solid-liquid separation, biological N removal from the liquid fraction, composting of the solid fraction) showed that higher NH3 and N2O emissions from the application of untreated manure lead to higher impacts for climate change and particulate matter formation (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018). Further research is needed to better un- derstand direct field emissions of other organic fertilizers beyond manure, compost, and digestate, and to integrate emission data into LCA. Besides, most of the mentioned comparisons were based on the amount of N applied but did not consider P and K application.

In this paper, soil organic carbon changes were assessed separately from the LCA. Although there is no standard procedure to account for soil C in LCAs, there have been attempts to analyse its contribution to the overall global warming potential. Different emission factors and datasets from literature can be used to estimate soil C changes after a land management shift (e.g. the input of crop residues or manure), the most used one being the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change emis- sion factor methodology (IPCC, 2019). Other authors instead use simple carbon models based on a site-dependent assessment, more complex dynamic crop-climate-soil models, or site-specific measurements (Goglio et al., 2015). Data for organic fertilizers other than manures or crop residues is difficult to find and requires further research. Once the amount of sequestered C is defined, it can be accounted for as a negative emission in LCA, but the duration of the storage is often not taken into account and there is a risk of overestimating its benefits (Brandão et al., 2013). The choice of time horizon is essential, since at some point all the C temporarily stored in the soil will be released back into the atmo- sphere. Applying a finite time horizon goes against the principle of intergenerational equity and the UN's 2030 Agenda. To avoid this without ignoring the benefits of increased soil organic C (e.g. main- taining soil structure, supporting microbial activity, preventing erosion, contributing to nutrient turnover) (Milne et al., 2015), the assessment was performed separately from the LCA. In this assessment, the LCA classification into constrained/unconstrained by-products and the consideration of marginal suppliers is not maintained (e.g. the SOCin- crease is calculated for manure, not for the marginal supplier mineral fertilizer). Although the "4 per mille" initiative goal cannot be achieved only by applying organic fertilizers at the investigated application rates (except for one scenario for rapeseed meal), it can be combined with other management practices, such as crop selection, crop rotation, change of bare fallow area, residue management, water management, etc.

67: The goal of this study was to explore the environmental benefits of replacing mineral fertilizers with conventional and innovative organic fertilizers. Results have shown that most of the commonly used organic fertilizers are constrained (the demand for an extra unit cannot be satisfied). Therefore, when planning the transition to a circular and organic agrifood system, as proposed by the European Commission, the focus should be put on untapped resources, such as microbial fertilizers (produced on wastewater from the food and beverage industry) and sewage sludge. Constrained markets vary with time and geographical location, decisions should hence be made after considering local con- ditions. In nutrient-intensive regions, such as Flanders, the application of excess manure is currently not constrained by resource availability, but it is by the EU Nitrates Regulation. A change in legislation would allow the use of this untapped resource. In this case, treating the manure is recommended to avoid field emissions.

The LCA results have shown that soybean meal and constrained fertilizers with soybean meal as marginal supplier (rapeseed meal, meat and bone meal, sugar beet molasse) often present high impacts across categories. These impacts are related to land use and land use change, emissions to air during cultivation, and depletion of natural gas. Although soybean meal is not yet constrained (increasing market), its use as fertilizer should be limited because land transformations from forest and other less-protected ecosystems to cropland contribute to climate change and biodiversity loss. Green manure is another plant- based fertilizer showing relatively high results linked to emissions dur- ing cultivation, land occupation, and depletion of fossil resources.

The impact of mineral fertilizers is lower than that of 15 organic fertilizers for the categories human health and ecosystem quality, and lower than that of 14 organic fertilizers for the category resource depletion (from a total of 17 organic fertilizers). Horn meal and dried sewage sludge perform better for all impact categories, and MaB for resource depletion. The differences between mineral and energy- intensive organic fertilizers decrease significantly in an industrial sym- biosis scenario, replacing fossil-based heat with waste heat from another process. The transport scenario also showed that applying locally available by-products as organic fertilizer would save transport-related impacts linked to imported mineral fertilizers. One advantage of organic fertilizers over mineral ones is that they add organic matter to the soil and contribute to carbon storage, mitigating climate change. SOC increase was not included in the LCA but was analysed separately. Although the "4 per mille" initiative goal cannot be achieved only by applying organic fertilizers (except for one scenario for rapeseed meal), it can be combined with other management practices, such as crop se- lection, crop rotation, change of bare fallow area, residue management, water management, etc. From the unconstrained fertilizers, green manure is the one with the highest contribution to the achievement of the goal (up to 95%).
